title: University of New Haven: Power On
body: Love the Video's this generation has in choosing a College/University!  In my time, you just had to visit, talk to people, maybe shadow a student, phone call, and figure things out.  Now the Advertisements to get high school students' interest blows me away.  It all really comes down to where you want to be and the professors you have.  I went to California State @ San Diego because I wanted to experience the Pacific Southwest.  And after my first year there, got savvy and chose all my professors who were top of their class in Ivy Leagues both undergrad and grad school. I felt I got an Ivy League education at a State run University just by carefully choosing my professors.  I had the BEST education at SDSU!
author: @maryvona7336

author: @rrandy_

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body: I live in New Haven, the complete disregard for the danger in this city that some of these kids display is appalling. It’s dangerous and we locals know that. The teenagers are out of control, the Kia boys are all over the place swinging in and out of traffic, and the drug use is completely out of control. The number of cops in the city is significantly decreased, we don’t see walking posts anymore. I’ve seen Yale students get forced to take money at the ATM then laugh it off as charity to make themselves feel better about being robbed. The false sense of security they have is complete delusion.
author: @Slowjo1221

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body:
New Haven gets a bad rap in terms of drugs, crime, etc. but it's pretty squarely average as far as cities of its size go. When you factor in how much of a toll redlining and urban renewal took on it, it's actually doing pretty well. It's also come a very, very long way in the last few years.

I would argue that New Haven has done a much, much better job than most places balancing cleaning up the city, and not pushing out long term residents with excessive gentrification. They're aiming for, "a rising tide lifts all boats" instead of, "push out the poor, and put in a Starbucks on every corner," and that's a much slower strategy. (And frankly, there are really no other cities of its size that boast as much culture, diversity, access to major metropolitan areas, etc.)
author: @Bunny-ch2ul

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body: Hello, Fair Haven Heights resident, here. Decent doc; one error: the “abandoned factories” right on the Quinnipiac River are neither abandoned nor factories. That shot was of an operational oil and jet fuel storage and distribution facility, and an fully functioning maritime maintenance business that fixes barges, etc. I walk by them daily (though not necessarily nightly).
author: @ostartsapostrophes

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body: Glad to see this covered. Was also struck by the disparity. Without Yale however, New Haven would be even worse off, like Lawrence or Waterbury. The problem really is the massive de-industrialization and lack of opportunities to re-skill or transition workers.
author: @prycedavies4828

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body: Yale has EXPLOITED it's (mostly) tax free status to essentially become a real estate business, buying up properties and claiming they are for education when really they are just there to make everything expensive for locals and pad that massive $40B endowment they have.  They aren't a school anymore.  Not by traditional definition.  They are a massive corporation that takes advantage of the city and the state.
author: @Hazdazos

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body:
As a graduate of both Penn and Princeton, I can say that the relationship those universities have with their towns is very different. Penn particularly takes an active interest in building up the surrounding environment around the University.

Yale has always had this total disregard for New Haven. I had a friend who took his PhD at Wisconsin-Madison and then went to Yale to do his postdoc. You can imagine his surprise at seeing not one but two drive-by shootings in the first 3 months.
author: @markbanash921

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body:
Yale is very safe. Even the surrounding area is safe.

But there are secrions of New Haven that are tough. Know where you are and where youre going and you'll be fine.
author: @Ninjanimegamer

title: Inside an Ivy League Hood (New Haven, CT)
body:
People blaming the college instead of the city management is crazy talk. It isn't the responsibility of a college to keep a city safe and functioning properly. Investing in the well being of the city falls on the leaders and elected officials.

Edit: sure seems to be a lot of you morons. The leaders and politicians are to blame for allowing this to happen. They’re in charge, they’ve allowed the college to do this. That means they’re failing the city and its residents. They continue to fail them.
author: @AshitakaYakul

body: Born and raised in New Haven and Yale was a 5 min walk from home . In my youth from1960 to 1973 I spend a lot of time on the campus , I didn't go to Yale but I had a few friends that were students , went of many mixers (dances ) because the university was open to the public .I wasn't till the drinking age was dropped that the younger generation messed it up for  Yale and city of New Haven relationship . I miss the old days
